The same model and prompt can therefore produce different completions depending on whether greedy decoding or sampling is used. This idea prepares the student for top-k and top-p sampling, which restrict the set of tokens before sampling.
index_text: Slide 50. Greedy vs random sampling repeated. Greedy always chooses highest probability word. Random weighted sampling can choose any token according to probabilities, creating varied outputs.

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces top-k and top-p sampling as two methods for controlling random generation. The interface again shows sample top K set to 25, sample top P set to 1, temperature set to 0.8, and max new tokens set to 200. Top-k and top-p are used after the model produces token probabilities. Their purpose is to restrict or shape the candidate token set before sampling, preventing the model from sampling from extremely unlikely tokens that might produce incoherent output. Top-k sampling keeps only the k highest-probability tokens. If k is 25, only the 25 most likely next tokens are considered, and all other vocabulary tokens are ignored for that step. Top-p sampling, also called nucleus sampling, keeps the smallest group of highest-probability tokens whose cumulative probability reaches p. If p is low, only a small high-confidence set is used; if p is 1, the full distribution may be allowed. These methods are important because pure random sampling over the entire vocabulary can sometimes select very unlikely tokens, while greedy decoding can be too rigid. Top-k and top-p offer middle ground: they preserve randomness and diversity but keep generation focused on plausible tokens. The slide prepares students for the next two slides, which explain each method visually.
index_text: Slide 51. Top-k and top-p sampling. Interface shows top K top P temperature and max new tokens. These parameters restrict candidate tokens before random sampling.

slide_paragraph: This slide explains top-k sampling using the same token probability distribution. The softmax output includes words such as cake, donut, banana, and apple with different probabilities. The slide defines top-k as selecting an output from the top k results after applying a random weighted strategy using the probabilities. In the example, k = 3, so only the three highest-probability tokens are kept as candidates. If the top three are cake, donut, and banana, then apple and the rest of the vocabulary are excluded from sampling for that step. The model then randomly samples among the remaining top-k tokens according to their probabilities, usually after renormalizing them so the remaining probabilities sum to 1. This gives a balance between greedy decoding and unrestricted sampling. Unlike greedy decoding, top-k sampling can choose something other than the single most probable token, which increases variation. Unlike unrestricted random sampling, it avoids very low-probability tokens that may be nonsensical. The choice of k matters. A small k makes generation more focused and deterministic, while a larger k allows more diversity but may introduce less likely words. This slide teaches that top-k is a simple rank-based filter: keep the k most likely tokens, then sample from them.
index_text: Slide 52. Top-k sampling. With k=3, select from the top three highest probability tokens after softmax using random weighted sampling. Lower-ranked tokens are excluded.

slide_paragraph: This slide explains top-p sampling, also called nucleus sampling. Instead of keeping a fixed number of tokens like top-k, top-p keeps the top-ranked consecutive tokens whose cumulative probability is less than or equal to a probability threshold p. The example shows p = 0.30. The token probabilities include cake at 0.20, donut at 0.10, banana at 0.02, apple at 0.01, and so on. Starting from the highest probability token, cake contributes 0.20. Adding donut brings the cumulative probability to 0.30. Because this reaches the threshold, the sampling set contains cake and donut, while banana, apple, and the rest are excluded for that step. The model then samples from the retained set according to their probabilities. The advantage of top-p is that the number of candidate tokens adapts to the shape of the probability distribution. If the model is very confident and the top tokens carry most probability mass, the candidate set is small. If the distribution is flatter, more tokens may be included to reach p. This makes top-p more flexible than top-k. The slide teaches that top-p controls diversity by probability mass rather than by a fixed count of tokens.
index_text: Slide 53. Top-p sampling. Select top-ranked consecutive tokens by probability until cumulative probability reaches p. Example p=0.30 includes cake 0.20 and donut 0.10.

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces temperature as another inference configuration parameter. The interface shows temperature set to 0.8 along with top K, top P, max new tokens, prompt input, and submit button. Temperature controls the shape of the probability distribution used for sampling. It does not change the model’s underlying knowledge, but it changes how strongly the model prefers high-probability tokens over lower-probability ones during decoding. A lower temperature makes the distribution sharper, meaning the highest-probability tokens become even more dominant. This usually produces more deterministic, focused, and conservative outputs. A higher temperature makes the distribution flatter, increasing the probability of less likely tokens and producing more diverse or creative outputs. A temperature around 1 often means the original distribution is used, while values below 1 reduce randomness and values above 1 increase randomness. The slide acts as an introduction before the next slide visually compares cooler and higher temperature distributions. For practical use, temperature is important because different tasks need different levels of creativity. Factual question answering, data extraction, and JSON formatting often benefit from low temperature. Brainstorming, creative writing, and ideation may benefit from higher temperature.
index_text: Slide 54. Temperature. Interface highlights temperature setting with top K top P and max new tokens. Temperature controls sharpness or flatness of token probabilities during generation.

slide_paragraph: This slide visually explains how temperature changes a model’s output probability distribution. On the left, a cooler temperature below 1 produces a strongly peaked distribution. In the example, cake has probability 0.400 while apple and banana have very small probabilities such as 0.001 and 0.002. This means the model is much more likely to choose the top token, making output more deterministic and focused. On the right, a higher temperature above 1 produces a broader, flatter distribution. The probabilities are more spread out: apple 0.040, banana 0.080, cake 0.150, donut 0.120, and so on. Cake may still be likely, but lower-ranked tokens now have a much better chance of being selected. The intuition is that temperature controls confidence during sampling. Lower temperature amplifies differences between logits, making the model behave more confidently. Higher temperature reduces differences, making the model more exploratory. This affects style and reliability. Low temperature is useful for precise tasks where the model should avoid unnecessary variation, such as extracting fields or answering factually. High temperature is useful when diversity is desired, such as generating multiple creative ideas. The slide reinforces that decoding parameters can significantly change outputs even when the prompt and model remain the same.
index_text: Slide 55. Temperature effect. Cooler temperature below 1 creates strongly peaked probabilities and more deterministic output. Higher temperature above 1 creates broader flatter probabilities and more diverse output.

slide_paragraph: This slide is a section divider introducing the generative AI project lifecycle. After discussing what generative AI and LLMs are, how language models work, transformer architectures, prompting, and inference parameters, the lecture now moves toward the practical process of building generative AI applications. A project lifecycle describes the sequence of decisions and stages needed to move from an idea to a working application. In generative AI, this usually includes defining the use case, selecting a model, adapting or aligning the model, evaluating it, deploying it efficiently, and integrating it into an application. Although this slide contains only the section title, it signals a shift from model concepts to engineering workflow. For students, this is important because successful generative AI projects are not built by choosing a model alone. They require clear task definition, data considerations, prompt or fine-tuning strategy, evaluation, inference optimization, and application integration.
index_text: Slide 56. Generative AI project lifecycle. Section divider introducing practical stages of building and deploying generative AI applications.

slide_title: Generative AI Project Lifecycle: From Scope to Application Integration
slide_paragraph: This slide presents a full lifecycle for building a generative AI project. The process begins with Scope, where the team defines the use case. This is the foundation because the model choice, data requirements, evaluation metrics, and deployment design all depend on what problem the application is meant to solve. The next stage is Select, where the team chooses an existing model or decides to pretrain its own. Choosing an existing model is common because pretraining from scratch is expensive, while pretraining a new model may be necessary for highly specialized or private domains. The central stage is Adapt and align model. It includes prompt engineering, fine-tuning, alignment with human feedback, and evaluation. Prompt engineering adjusts the instructions and context without changing model weights. Fine-tuning updates the model on task-specific data. Alignment with human feedback improves behavior according to human preferences, safety requirements, or domain standards. Evaluation checks whether the model performs well and reliably. The final stage is Application integration. It includes optimizing and deploying the model for inference, then augmenting the model and building LLM-powered applications. Optimization may involve quantization, batching, caching, or choosing efficient serving infrastructure. Augmentation may include retrieval systems, tools, APIs, or external applications. The slide teaches that generative AI development is an end-to-end lifecycle, not just model training.
index_text: Slide 57. Generative AI project lifecycle. Stages: define use case, choose or pretrain model, adapt and align with prompt engineering fine-tuning human feedback and evaluation, optimize and deploy for inference, augment model and build LLM applications.

slide_paragraph: This slide appears to be a continuation or section marker for the generative AI project lifecycle. The visible text contains the lifecycle title but does not provide additional detailed bullet points or diagrams. Based on its placement after the full lifecycle diagram, it likely prepares the lecture to discuss one or more lifecycle stages in more detail, such as defining a use case, choosing between general and specialized models, or integrating models into applications. The important educational idea is that generative AI projects should be approached systematically. A model must be matched to a task, adapted if needed, evaluated against meaningful criteria, and integrated into a usable system. Even though no extra visual details are visible on this slide, students should treat it as part of the transition from conceptual LLM knowledge to practical development methodology.
index_text: Slide 58. Generative AI project lifecycle continuation. Visible content indicates the lifecycle section continues, likely focusing on project stages and practical development.

slide_title: Good at Many Tasks or a Single Task?
slide_paragraph: This slide contrasts general-purpose generative AI capability with task-specific application behavior. The title asks whether a model is good at many tasks or at a single task. The slide lists tasks such as essay writing, summarization, translation, and information retrieval, showing that modern LLMs can often perform many language tasks using the same underlying model. This is one of the major strengths of large pretrained models: instead of training a separate model for every task, a single model can be prompted to write, summarize, translate, retrieve, explain, or answer questions. On the right, the slide also shows action calls, APIs, external applications, and “invoke APIs and actions.” This indicates that LLM-powered systems can go beyond text generation by calling tools or interacting with external software. For example, an assistant may call a search API, query a database, send an email, retrieve a document, or perform an action in another application. The educational message is that generative AI applications can be designed in two ways. One approach uses the model as a general-purpose multi-task engine. Another approach specializes the system for a particular task or connects it to tools so it can act in the real world. The slide prepares students to think about LLMs as components inside larger applications rather than isolated text generators.
index_text: Slide 59. Good at many tasks or a single task. LLMs can support essay writing summarization translation and information retrieval. They can also invoke APIs and actions through external applications.

slide_paragraph: This slide is another continuation slide for the generative AI project lifecycle section. The visible text only shows the lifecycle title, so no specific process details are available on the slide itself. Its placement after the discussion of many-task versus single-task systems suggests that the lecture may be continuing to discuss practical application design choices. In a generative AI lifecycle, such choices include whether to use a general model as-is, specialize it with prompts or fine-tuning, augment it with retrieval or tools, or deploy it as part of a larger workflow. The slide therefore functions as a transition point. The main idea students should retain is that building generative AI systems involves repeated decisions across scope, model choice, adaptation, evaluation, and integration. Without additional visible content, it is important not to infer a specific technique beyond the general lifecycle theme.
index_text: Slide 60. Generative AI project lifecycle continuation. Visible title only; likely continues practical discussion of application design and lifecycle decisions.

slide_paragraph: This slide continues the generative AI project lifecycle section and contains only the visible title. Because no bullets, diagram components, or equations are visible, the safest interpretation is that it serves as a transition or placeholder within the lecture. In the context of the surrounding slides, it likely belongs to the practical part of the lecture that explains how generative AI systems move from a defined use case to deployed applications. The lifecycle perspective reminds students that there are multiple stages: define the problem, select or train a model, adapt it through prompting or fine-tuning, align it with human expectations, evaluate it, optimize it for inference, and integrate it with applications or tools. This slide itself does not add new technical details, but it reinforces the organizational theme of the section.
index_text: Slide 61. Generative AI project lifecycle continuation. Visible title only; reinforces lifecycle workflow for generative AI systems.

slide_paragraph: This final slide lists the references used to prepare the lecture. The first reference is “Hands-On Large Language Models” by Jay Alammar and Maarten Grootendorst, which is a practical resource for understanding LLM concepts, architectures, and applications. The second reference is DeepLearning.AI’s “Generative AI for Everyone” course, which suggests that some high-level explanations of generative AI applications and project lifecycle concepts may be inspired by that course. The third reference is an “Introduction to Large Language Model (LLM)” lecture by Ekta Vats, likely used for foundational LLM definitions and architecture explanations. The fourth reference is “A brief introduction to (large) language models” by Sachin Kumar, likely supporting the probability, language modeling, and historical development parts of the lecture. This slide is important academically because it shows that the lecture content is grounded in existing educational sources rather than presented without attribution. For students, the references provide follow-up material if they want to deepen their understanding of generative AI, language modeling, transformer architectures, prompting, and LLM application development. The slide also signals the end of the lecture and identifies resources that can be used for review, assignments, or further study.
index_text: Slide 62. Lecture References. Sources include Hands-On Large Language Models by Jay Alammar and Maarten Grootendorst, DeepLearning.AI Generative AI for Everyone, Ekta Vats LLM lecture, and Sachin Kumar language models lecture.
